name: Fats Waller
abstract: Thomas Wright "Fats" Waller (May 21, 1904 – December 15, 1943) was an American jazz pianist, organist, composer, and singer. His innovations in the stride style were widely influential among musicians.

concerts.label: W. C. Handy's Orchestra and Jubilee Singers

works.t: Variations on a Theme by George Gershwin

about_quotes.speaker: Terry Clark
about_quotes.evidence: But the most popular person at that time, jazz-wise, small group was Fats Waller... So that kind of – I was thinking about that, back in those days when we were trying to get our little group going, back in St. Louis, back in the ’30s
about_quotes.doc_title: Terry Clark — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Phil Schaap
about_quotes.evidence: I said, “Well that’s Count Basie playing a little combination Fats Waller and four hands in his early records with Benny Moten’s Kansas City Orchestra, of ‘Jones Law Blues’ and ‘Small Black’”
about_quotes.doc_title: Phil Schaap interviewed by Monk Rowe, Toronto, Canada, January 9, 2003

about_quotes.speaker: Keith Ingham
about_quotes.evidence: people like Benny Carter, Duke Ellington, Art Tatum, I mean Fats Waller... I think they had that thing... can you imagine Fats Waller, the piano player, you know you’ve seen him in movies
about_quotes.doc_title: Keith Ingham interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 12, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Cliff Leeman
about_quotes.evidence: Hank Duncan worked there also... he used to go out on the road when Fats Waller had two pianos. Nobody knows. That's why Hank adopted a lot of Fats Waller songs.

about_quotes.speaker: Billy Taylor
about_quotes.evidence: I did 55 minutes on Count Basie... where Count Basie talked about Count Basie... how he sat at the feet of Fats Waller, and how he learned to play the organ
about_quotes.doc_title: Billy Taylor interviewed by Michael Woods, New York City, New York, July 27, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Gary Smulyan
about_quotes.evidence: I was just flipping the radio dial and heard Fats Waller playing “African Ripples,” which is a solo piano piece, and I’d never heard anything like that.
